chapters.title: Accelerated trading speed on Wall Street
chapters.text:
There is a big difference between the trading speed offered by exchange companies and the speed that is theoretically possible.

The speed of light in fiber allows a trader to send an order to New York from Chicago and back in 12 milliseconds.

In practice, exchange companies take a longer time — a speed of as much as 16 milliseconds was once recorded. The fastest route traders have been able to access moves at 14.65 milliseconds on what they refer to as The Gold Route. If you find this route by some chance, you will be the first to profit from discrepancies between the prices in Chicago and those in New York.

Telecom markets could not comprehend the importance of a millisecond. For instance, the telecom company Verizon had no idea their connection speed was a valuable asset. The fact that their route moved two milliseconds faster made them worth a fortune. The variation in connection speed was because the date route moved in a zig-zag pattern across roads and railroads. A straight, wired path between Chicago and New York would yield a greater connection speed.

Hence, Dan Spivey, a trader on the Chicago Board Options Exchange, set out to achieve this himself. He got the necessary permissions and funds to start company spread networks. The one thing he never anticipated was that traders at Wall Street would not appreciate the importance of a millisecond and, as a result, would not want to buy their line. The success of Verizon’s product depended heavily on the scarcity of faster connections.

By 2010, Verizon was still constructing; they didn’t have a single prospective buyer. They kept their operation a secret to maximize the line’s value and prevent copycats from replicating their work. They waited three months to the completion date before advertising and contacted the most highly paid men on Wall Street, and the first reaction of many was total disbelief.

In the remaining chapters of this summary, you will discover how computer programming and connection speeds became technological tools that gave undue advantage to traders who knew how to leverage them. Also, you’ll see how the telecommunication industry got rich because of Wall Street. The toll trading speeds took on the stock market and how insider trading disrupted the ebb and flow of the institution are also duly addressed.

chapters.title: The danger of slow market data
chapters.text:
Brad Katsuyama was employed by the Royal Bank of Canada (RBC), which, despite being the ninth-largest bank globally, did not hold much significance on Wall Street. In 2002, he relocated from Toronto to New York, where he gained a reputation as a standout performer at RBC.

Katsuyama's challenges emerged in late 2006 following RBC's acquisition of the U.S. electronic stock market trading firm Carlin Financial for $100 million. This acquisition compelled him to adopt Carlin's technology. On his screen, he observed an offering of 10,000 shares of Intel at $22 per share. However, any attempt to interact with the computer resulted in the disappearance of the displayed information.

A stock market that appears on your screen is illusory, shaped by millisecond-level changes.

Facing technical issues with his computer, Katsuyama sought assistance from tech support. When they claimed he was the problem, not the machine, he invited developers to witness as he traded. The offerings vanished at his command, causing a stock surge and leaving the developers at a loss for an explanation.

Someone out there was using the fact that stock market orders arrived at different times at different exchanges to front-run orders from one market to another. ~ Michael Lewis

Katsuyama recognized that his trades were being front-run, with another trader exploiting his demand by buying on different exchanges before selling it back to him at a higher price. He suspected high-frequency traders.

When the developers investigated his computer issues, they attributed the problem to Katsuyama's location in New York and the markets being in New Jersey, asserting that the distance caused slow market data. They explained that the complexity arose from the multitude of people actively trading in the market, numbering in the hundreds or even thousands. So, it was apparent that speed had become invaluable to trading.

Did you know? Wall Street is the largest stock exchange in the world. According to the Wall Street Journal, average daily trading was around $467 billion on single-stock options in 2021.

chapters.title: The automation of stock markets
chapters.text:
Ronan Ryan was an Irish immigrant who came to the United States in 1990. His parents were not well-to-do, but they did their best. He got his high school education from a private school in Greenwich, after which he was offered a job in the New York office of MCI Communications. There, he learned about technology, which helped him understand the underlying mechanics behind the telecommunication system and the working principles of the industry inside out. He knew how information moved between different locations; it traveled in a zig-zag path from one telecom carrier to the next. For instance, during a call from New York to Florida, information moves through multiple pieces of equipment.

The U.S. stock market shifted from having trading skills to having trading speeds that could execute trades in nanoseconds.

Communication data does not follow a straight path, as most people think. The circuit that connects New York City to Florida moves through Verizon, BellSouth, and MCI in a zig-zag path. The time it takes for signals to move through such paths is called latency and is determined by the boxes, logic, and lines. The boxes are the machinery held within these telecommunication companies, and the logic relates to the coding on which the boxes operate. The lines referred to the glass fiber-optic cables that transmit information from one box to another.

The U.S. financial markets had always been either corrupt or about to be corrupted. ~ Michael Lewis

The fiber length used by these companies was the most significant determinant of latency. Knowing the mechanics of latency made Ryan a valuable asset in the telecommunication industry because everyone wanted to know how they could execute trades faster than others.

Ronan Ryan knew how to achieve higher levels of latency. As a result, Brad Katsuyama hired him and put him in charge of the high-frequency trading strategies unit. The agreement was that while Katsuyama taught Ryan to trade, Ryan was to teach Katsuyama technology.

chapters.title: Putting a face on high-frequency trading (HFT)
chapters.text:
Sergey Aleynikov was an extremely gifted computer programmer, capable of writing codes that passed huge amounts of information at great speed. He was contacted by a headhunter who informed him of the need for his skill level on Wall Street. Aleynikov's time was an important moment in the history of Wall Street, as the American stock market fragmentation began. This change was stimulated by the Regulation National Market System (Reg NMS), a U.S. initiative to strengthen the National Market System, which is also responsible for considerable stock market trading.

Sergey Aleynikov joined Goldman Sachs to solve three major issues. First, he was to create a means by which translated data from the 13 public exchanges could be viewed as coming from a single stream.

In the market, decisions are now solely made by computers, removing human responsibility.

The second task was to acquire information for the private ticker plant and use it to discover smart trades. He had to change the codes that ran the software before they became any faster.

The third task, “order entry,” was creating software for trade execution. At the time, Aleynikov didn't know that he was building a high-frequency trading firm for Goldman Sachs.

After putting in months of hard work, Aleynikov told the management of Goldman Sachs that the only way they could achieve high-frequency trading was to scrap the existing platform completely and build a new one from scratch. However, management was not interested in carrying out this suggestion; their focus was on short-term goals, and this was a risk, as Wall Street was increasingly becoming focused on sophisticated platforms.

chapters.title: Wall Street often offers no loyalty
chapters.text:
Sergey Aleynikov was reputed to be one of the best programmers of his time. He was needed in multiple high-frequency firms and greatly impacted many businesses and their success on Wall Street. During the financial crisis of 2008, he was offered a job at another high-frequency trading firm, but he did not want to leave Goldman Sachs. He was comfortable right where he was, and when they matched the offer, he decided to stay.

Eventually, Aleynikov was made another offer to build a new trading platform from scratch, and the proposal was much higher. The money did not move him, but the prospect of building a new platform from scratch really excited him. He was offered a million-dollar annual salary and a posting to a branch closer to his home.

He took the job and quit Goldman Sachs. Many of his co-workers offered to accompany him, and his bosses tried to persuade him to stay. They offered him a larger paycheck, but he turned it down.

After taking a flight to Chicago, he noticed three men dressed in black. They walked up to him and introduced themselves as the FBI. He was arrested that same day for stealing the computer code owned by Goldman Sachs.

He spoke to his wife that night and encouraged her to allow the FBI to search his home, even though they went in with no search warrant. The FBI had tampered with his confession, making the jury believe he was being cautious and tricky with his words.

Consequently, the judge sentenced him to eight years in federal prison without any possibility of parole.

Exercise caution and seek legal counsel before consenting to searches or making statements to law enforcement.

chapters.title: The financial ecosystem playground
chapters.text:
In 2008, it occurred to Brad Katsuyama that the stock market had changed into something else. It was dominated by companies with technical gadgets that could manipulate speed deficiencies to their advantage. This made him seek technically gifted people.

He began to put together a team of technologically gifted youngsters. The first member of this team was a young newcomer named Rob Park. He put a lot of effort into making his first choice because Park was to be a part of the selection process from then on. The second member of the team, Dan Aisen, was discovered from a pile of resumes at RBC; at the time, he was nicknamed the puzzle master.

Katsuyama was so impressed with Aisen's work that he asked for more puzzle champions. Aisen then recommended his teammate Francis Chung, and Katsuyama offered Chung a job as the last member of the team. The team's goal was not to outsmart other traders but to create a haven where people could trade without being preyed upon by high-frequency traders.

The puzzle masters identified three activities that contributed to the unfair trading experience traders suffered at the time. The first was the one Katsuyama encountered from earlier — front running.

Front running happens when high-frequency traders predict and act on your trades ahead of you.

Rebate arbitrage was the second fraudulent trading activity they recognized. This activity involved using the increased complexity of Wall Street to make trades without providing the corresponding liquidity.

The third and most problematic trick identified was “slow market arbitrage.” This occurred when a high-frequency trader could observe changes in stock exchange rates on one machine and execute transactions on another.

All three activities had one thing in common — they required high speed.

They wanted to create a space within which speed would not give a trader an edge over others. To accomplish this, they established the Investors Exchange (IEX). The idea was to make all trading transactions at this point and keep everyone at an equivalent distance away from the location of the IES. This eliminated most of the advantages posed by speed.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Our perspective of the stock market hasn't changed much over the years, and we tend to hold tight to our old-fashioned view because it's hard to understand how complex the market is today. The market has become dominated by technology and tactics, and much of it is so cutthroat that loyalty is a thing of the past. This guide, built from a collection of smaller pictures of the post-crisis Wall Street, shows the world's biggest stock market in a light that many people may not expect.

The speed of trading has become more important than understanding trading itself. Internet service providers who could offer traders breakneck trading speed make a lot of money on Wall Street.

Wall Street firms are usually unwilling to open up about what is happening within them. Algorithms executing trades that would otherwise have been impossible for a human to implement are everywhere. Anyone looking to join the fray must spend more time and resources on programming than they do on understanding the market. Artificial intelligence and quantum computing have become the magic wand for the big dogs to control the markets.

Nobody can predict what the stock market will become in the years we face. And this is partly because nobody predicted the changes that occurred over the last few decades. However, technology is sure to play a huge part, and with new developments happening all the time, it's likely to be a very sophisticated stock market when we look back at it in a decade or two.

Try this



• If you’re new to the stock market, it might be helpful to place more emphasis on the internet speed available to you.
• Also, avoid high-frequency trading as a beginner.
• Opt for the Investors Exchange (IEX) because it provides an alternative to high-frequency trading.

Read it till the end?
